---
suppress-bibliography: true
---

# Results

## Study population

The included study population had a mean age of 66 years (SD = 7.1) and 53% were men. The mean BMI was 27 kg/m² (SD = 4.5) and the majority were current smokers (63%). Glycemic categories were distributed as follows: 734 (53%) NGT, 249 (18%) iIFG, 122 (8.8%) iIGT, 116 (8.4%) IFG+IGT and 160 (12%) type 2 diabetes. Regarding body fat distribution, women had higher body fat percentage and SAT than men, while men had higher VAT and VAT/SAT ratio. Participants excluded from the clustering analysis were more likely to have higher BMI, waist circumference, VAT and HbA1c compared to the included participants. Participants excluded from the regression analysis were more likely to have type 2 diabetes, impaired glucose tolerance and had higher BMI compared to the included participants.

## K-means clustering

### Clustering solution and characteristics

A three-cluster solution was identified as the most appropriate clustering solution based on statistical criteria, distinct OGTT-profiles and sufficient cluster sizes, with an overall silhouette width of 0.21. While the NbClust package indicated highest support for two and nine clusters, a two-cluster solution collapsed the incretin and glucagon heterogeneity into a single group dominated by glucose and insulin differences, and solutions with more than three clusters produced very small clusters (n=2) driven by extreme values on glucagon suppression. Cluster characteristics of the three-cluster solution are presented in table 1. Cluster 1 (n = 459) had the youngest mean age, highest proportion of males and highest physical activity level, while cluster 2 (n = 491) had the highest proportion of women and cluster 3 (n = 431) had the oldest mean age and highest proportion of current smokers. Regarding adiposity, Cluster 3 had the highest BMI, waist circumference, body fat percentage, and VAT in both men and women, while Cluster 2 had the lowest BMI, waist circumference, and SAT, and Cluster 1 had the lowest VAT and VAT/SAT ratio. The distribution of glycemic status differed markedly across clusters. Cluster 3 had the highest proportion of type 2 diabetes (33%), IFG+IGT (25%) and iIGT (21%), while the majority of individuals in cluster 1 and 2 had normoglycemia (69% and 78%, respectively). Sex-stratified clustering (supplementary table 1) produced three similar clusters in both men and women, with consistent within and between-cluster differences, similar to the main analysis.

An overview of clustering variables across the three clusters is shown in figure 1. The temporal OGTT response patterns underlying these clusters are shown in figure 2, with mean response curves for glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon (*Fig. 2A, B, C, D* and *E,* respectively) at all three time points (0, 30 and 120 minutes).

Cluster 3 is characterized by elevated glucose levels throughout the OGTT, with the highest fasting glucose (6.5 mmol/L), and 2hPG (9.1 mmol/L) alongside the highest insulin concentrations, with a fasting insulin of 53.7 pmol/L and 30 minute and 2-hour insulin levels of 250 pmol/L and 389.2 pmol/L, respectively. This corresponds to the lowest value on the insulin sensitivity index across clusters (ISI = 21). In contrast, cluster 2 had the lowest 2hPG (5.4 mmol/L) and a higher peak in insulin at 30 minutes (214 pmol/L) compared to cluster 1 (180 pmol/L), with a corresponding highest value on the insulin sensitivity index of 48 compared to 42 for cluster 1. Cluster 1 had the lowest incretin secretion (AUC<sub>0-120</sub> = 1,873 and 3,859 pmol/L × min for GLP-1 and GIP, respectively), with substantially lower incretin secretion compared to cluster 2 (AUC<sub>0-120</sub> = 3,938 and 6,265 pmol/L × min for GLP-1 and GIP, respectively). Furthermore, cluster 3 showed an early increase in glucagon (0-30 min suppression = -10.3%), but the highest late glucagon suppression (30-120 min, 44.4%), and cluster 2 had lower glucagon suppression than cluster 1 across all intervals. Despite the differences in early and late response patterns, cluster 1 and 3 had similar overall glucagon suppression (47% and 45.3%, respectively), with cluster 3 having the highest glucagon secretion (AUC <sub>0-120 min</sub> = 1,081 pmol/L × min). Detailed OGTT measurements are shown in supplementary table 2.

### Associations to measures of fat distribution

Estimates from all four outcome models, representing associations between cluster membership and measures of body fat distribution, are shown in table 2 and reported as mean difference in SD units. In the model adjusted for age and sex, cluster 3 had higher VAT (0.68 SD, 95% CI 0.56, 0.81), SAT (0.28 SD, 95% CI 0.15, 0.42), VAT/SAT ratio (0.18 SD, 95% CI 0.06, 0.31) and body fat percentage (0.63 SD, 95% CI: 0.52, 0.73) compared to cluster 2. Cluster 1 showed higher SAT (0.25 SD, 95% CI: 0.12, 0.38), while we found no evidence of a difference in VAT compared to cluster 2 (-0.03 SD, 95% CI: -0.15, 0.09). Sex-specific associations are illustrated in supplementary figure 1. No sex-by-cluster interactions were observed (p \> 0.05), and interaction terms were therefore not included in the main models, although the largest sex differences were observed in SAT and VAT/SAT ratio for cluster 1. Estimates were largely unchanged after further adjustment for smoking and physical activity. Regression models with original values can be found in supplementary table 3.
